Adapting to Behavioral Styles Participant Reference Guide

Welcome to the Workshop!

Today, we embark on an interactive journey to master the art of adaptive communication. You will not just be presenting; you will be building stronger, more effective relationships that lead to greater success in every customer call. Let's get ready to play, learn, and grow!

Workshop Objectives

1

Understand the concept of Behavioral Styles and the two-axis model behind it.

2

Learn the key characteristics, values, and priorities of the four main styles: Driver, Expressive, Analytical, and Social.

3

Develop the skill to quickly identify a customer's style through verbal and non-verbal cues.

4

Master strategies to adapt your communication and sales approach to each specific style.

5

Apply these skills through practical case studies and real-world interaction scenarios.

6

Create a personal action plan to translate today's learning into tomorrow's results.

Today's Agenda

  • Module 1: The World of Behavioral Styles An introduction to the "why" and "what" of behavioral patterns.
  • Module 2: Discovering You A hands-on self-assessment to uncover your personal communication style.
  • Module 3: Meet the Four Styles A deep dive into the Driver, Expressive, Analytical, and Social styles.
  • Module 4: The Art of Adaptation How to flex your style for maximum impact with each customer type.
  • Module 5: Practice Arena Case studies and call-planning exercises to apply your new skills.
  • Module 6: Your Personal Action Plan Translating today's learnings into tomorrow's actions.
The Behavioral Styles Model
Understanding the two fundamental scales of human behavior
Behavioral styles are predictable patterns of behavior — not judgments of good or bad. When you understand these patterns, you can predict how a customer will respond and adapt your approach for maximum impact.
Scale 1

Assertiveness

Describes a person's level of directness and forcefulness in relating to others.

Low
Ask-oriented
Listens more
High
Tell-oriented
Speaks more

High assertiveness = more forceful, direct, quick to act. Low assertiveness = more thoughtful, asks rather than tells, slower to decide.

Scale 2

People / Task Orientation

Describes whether a person's primary focus is on people or on tasks and processes.

Task
Logic-driven
Facts-first
People
Feelings-driven
Relations-first

Task-oriented = focuses on data, process, outcomes. People-oriented = focuses on feelings, relationships, collaboration.

The Four Quadrants

Key Insight: Reflect for a Moment…

  • 💭 When you meet a new customer for the first time, how quickly do you form an impression about whether you can trust them?
  • 💭 Which patterns of behavior make you doubt or believe in an individual's trustworthiness?
  • 💭 Think about a customer you know well. With what degree of accuracy can you predict how they will respond to different situations?
Meet the Four Styles
Deep-dive into each style's values, priorities, and behavioral cues
The Driver
High Assertiveness Task-Focused
  • 🎯
    Values: Initiative, control, immediate results
  • ⚡
    Priority: Power and utility — "What's in it for me and how does it get me to my goal?"
  • 🔍
    Focus: Objectives, bottom line, the here and now. Results over process.
  • 🛡️
    Under Pressure: Intimidation — becomes aggressive, may try to overpower the situation.
  • 🏆
    Wants to be seen as: Productive, in control, accomplished.
🕵️ Body Language Cues ▼
  • Pace/Movement: Deliberate, quick, controlled.
  • Posture: Erect, formal; often leans forward; may place elbows on the table.
  • Gestures: Direct (e.g., pointing), intense, serious face.
  • Eye Contact: Direct and sustained — holds your gaze.
  • Speech/Voice: Uses voice for emphasis; can be loud and forceful. Few pauses.
The Expressive
High Assertiveness People-Focused
  • 🌟
    Values: Stimulating, inspiring, and persuading others.
  • 🏅
    Priority: Admiration, recognition, setting big aspirations. They love to be the first.
  • 🔭
    Focus: Dreams, a better future, the big picture. They are natural visionaries.
  • 🥊
    Under Pressure: Fight — may become argumentative or overly dramatic.
  • ✨
    Wants to be seen as: Creative, innovative, admired, and ahead of the curve.
🕵️ Body Language Cues ▼
  • Pace/Movement: Almost constant motion, fast pace, whole body moves, very fluid.
  • Posture: Open and relaxed; comfortable touching and being touched.
  • Gestures: Sweeping, dramatic; very expressive face with lots of eye movement.
  • Eye Contact: Direct and personal, seeks to connect emotionally.
  • Speech/Voice: Very fluent, frequent voice inflection, animated and sometimes loud.
The Analytical
Low Assertiveness Task-Focused
  • 🔬
    Values: Accuracy, analysis, and logical reasoning.
  • 📊
    Priority: Precision, efficiency, sticking to procedure. They want to be right.
  • 📋
    Focus: Facts, logic, details, evidence, documentation — proof before decisions.
  • 🐢
    Under Pressure: Flight — withdraws, goes silent, or buries themselves in data to avoid confrontation.
  • 🎓
    Wants to be seen as: Credible, thorough, evidence-based, and correct.
🕵️ Body Language Cues ▼
  • Pace/Movement: Slow, limited, purposeful — minimal wasted motion.
  • Posture: Can seem stiff or uncomfortable; often leans back, maintains distance.
  • Gestures: Does not readily reveal emotions; classic "poker face."
  • Eye Contact: Intermittent — may look away or upward while processing.
  • Speech/Voice: Precise and deliberate with frequent pauses; quietly spoken, little inflection.
The Social
Low Assertiveness People-Focused
  • 🤝
    Values: Direct contact, genuine personal relationships, human connection.
  • 🕊️
    Priority: Approval and harmony — they want everyone to get along and feel heard.
  • 💛
    Focus: Relationships, personal feelings, team cohesion, emotional well-being.
  • 🌊
    Under Pressure: Backs down — accommodates to avoid conflict, even at personal cost.
  • 🌱
    Wants to be seen as: Caring, trustworthy, a good person who genuinely helps others.
🕵️ Body Language Cues ▼
  • Pace/Movement: Slow, relaxed, easy pace — never rushed.
  • Posture: Leans back, relaxed and informal; comfortable with closeness in known company.
  • Gestures: Friendly face, "smiling" eyes, readily shows emotions and empathy.
  • Eye Contact: Intermittent but warm — used to signal connection and approval.
  • Speech/Voice: Quiet voice, slower pace; warm and gentle tone, often pauses to listen.
The Art of Adaptation
How to flex your communication style for maximum impact
Understanding the styles is only half the battle. The real skill is adapting your approach in real time. Use the dialogue examples below to recognize each style in conversation — then apply the Do/Don't strategies to build trust and open doors.

Adapting to the Driver

High Assertiveness · Task-Oriented

✅ DO

  • Be direct — state your purpose immediately
  • Focus on results, efficiency, and ROI
  • Give options, let them decide
  • Use data that shows clear outcomes
  • Respect their time — be brief
  • Ask for commitment directly

❌ DON'T

  • Small talk or personal chit-chat
  • Pad out your message with fluff
  • Make decisions for them
  • Be disorganized or unprepared
  • Over-explain the process
  • Show hesitation or weakness

💬 How a Driver Sounds

"I have 5 minutes. What's the key benefit of this product compared to what I'm currently using — and what's the evidence?"
"Understood. Three key advantages: faster onset, lower discontinuation rate based on the Phase III data, and fewer follow-up visits — which means less burden on your schedule."

Adapting to the Expressive

High Assertiveness · People-Oriented

✅ DO

  • Open with enthusiasm and energy
  • Appeal to vision — "be first," "be ahead"
  • Use storytelling and patient narratives
  • Give genuine recognition and compliments
  • Show how this sets them apart
  • Allow them to share their opinions

❌ DON'T

  • Lead with dense data tables
  • Be overly formal or stiff
  • Dominate the conversation
  • Rush past their ideas and stories
  • Focus only on details and processes
  • Ignore their need for recognition

💬 How an Expressive Sounds

"You know, I was just thinking — none of my colleagues have tried this approach yet. What do you think — could this make a real difference for my patients?"
"That's exactly the kind of forward-thinking I've been seeing from the early adopters in your field. Your patients would feel the difference, and frankly, this positions you ahead of the trend."

Adapting to the Analytical

Low Assertiveness · Task-Oriented

✅ DO

  • Lead with evidence and solid data
  • Be structured, accurate, and prepared
  • Provide details and documentation
  • Give them time to think — don't rush
  • Acknowledge their expertise
  • Offer data to take away and review

❌ DON'T

  • Be vague or make unsupported claims
  • Rush or pressure for quick decisions
  • Be overly informal or "salesy"
  • Show up without the full data
  • Skip the methodology
  • Dismiss or simplify their questions

💬 How an Analytical Sounds

"What was the sample size? And was this study conducted in a comparable patient population to mine? I'd need to review the full methodology before forming an opinion."
"Absolutely, Dr. [Name]. The study had 1,200 patients — I have the full publication here. I'll also leave the data appendix so you can review the sub-group analysis at your convenience."

Adapting to the Social

Low Assertiveness · People-Oriented

✅ DO

  • Start with genuine personal connection
  • Focus on patient comfort and well-being
  • Show empathy and listen actively
  • Offer reassurance and support
  • Use patient stories and testimonials
  • Build trust before asking for commitment

❌ DON'T

  • Jump straight into product features
  • Be aggressive or push for quick decisions
  • Be cold, transactional, or rushed
  • Ignore their concern for others
  • Challenge them publicly
  • Make them feel alone in a decision

💬 How a Social Sounds

"I just want to make sure my patients are comfortable and that they won't have any worrying side effects. Their trust in me means everything — I can't risk that."
"I completely understand — patient trust is everything. That's actually why many doctors like yourself have found this option reassuring: the tolerability profile is very gentle, and your patients are very likely to report a positive experience back to you."
Practice Arena
Apply your knowledge with real-world call planning scenarios
For each case, read the doctor profile, identify the dominant style, and complete your call plan. These scenarios are representative of typical customer interactions — adapt the context to your own territory and product.
🎯 Driver Style

Dr. Kareem — The Results-Driven Specialist

Dr. Kareem is a senior specialist at a busy hospital. He runs his department with military efficiency and is known for being direct, time-conscious, and focused on outcomes. He has a heavy patient load and values representatives who come prepared, get to the point, and provide clear proof of value. He will not tolerate vague claims or wasted time.

📝 Call Plan Worksheet — Dr. Kareem (Driver)

1 My Primary Objective for this Call
2 My Planned Opening Statement
Hint: Be direct. State your purpose in the first sentence. Reference a relevant outcome or data point immediately.
3 Key Messages & Benefits to Highlight
Hint: Focus on results, efficiency, competitive advantage, and how it saves him time or effort.
4 Probing Questions to Ask
Hint: Ask closed or single-focus questions that help you confirm his main challenge and align your product to his specific goal.
5 How I Will Ask for Commitment
Hint: Be clear and propose a specific, logical next step. Offer two options for him to choose from.
🔬 Analytical Style

Dr. Hesham — The Evidence-Based Researcher

Dr. Hesham is an academic specialist at a university teaching hospital. He is deeply involved in research and prides herself on evidence-based practice. Before adopting any new product, he requires all clinical data, including methodology and sub-group analyses. He is formal, precise, and will ask very detailed questions. He will not be rushed into a decision.

📝 Call Plan Worksheet — Dr. Hesham (Analytical)

1 My Primary Objective for this Call
2 My Planned Opening Statement
Hint: Be formal and reference data or a specific clinical finding. Acknowledge his expertise first.
3 Key Messages & Benefits to Highlight
Hint: Lead with clinical evidence, study quality, methodology, and patient safety data. Be specific and accurate.
4 Probing Questions to Ask
Hint: Ask open questions about his current evaluation criteria and what data he would need to feel confident in a recommendation.
5 How I Will Ask for Commitment
Hint: Offer a low-pressure next step — provide data to review, offer a follow-up meeting after he has had time to evaluate.
🌟 Expressive Style

Dr. Sara — The Visionary Innovator

Dr. Sara runs a high-profile private practice and is widely recognized as a leader in her field. She is an early adopter, loves being ahead of the curve, and has a charismatic, enthusiastic personality. She cares about her reputation and enjoys sharing her experiences and opinions. She values relationships with representatives who bring her exciting new ideas and treat her as a partner.

📝 Call Plan Worksheet — Dr. Sara (Expressive)

1 My Primary Objective for this Call
2 My Planned Opening Statement
Hint: Be enthusiastic and open with something that appeals to her vision, her leadership, or her desire to be ahead of the trend.
3 Key Messages & Benefits to Highlight
Hint: Focus on innovation, the "wow factor," how this makes her stand out, and the patient experience story.
4 Probing Questions to Ask
Hint: Ask open questions that invite her to share her opinions, experiences, and vision for her practice.
5 How I Will Ask for Commitment
Hint: Frame it as a partnership — invite her to be one of the first to try it and share her experience. Make her feel special.
🤝 Social Style

Dr. Hana — The Empathetic Caregiver

Dr. Hana works in a community clinic and is adored by her patients for her warmth and empathy. She takes extra time to listen and considers the personal circumstances of each patient. Her primary concern is always patient well-being and safety above all else. She values long-term relationships and is loyal to representatives she trusts. She is slow to commit but deeply loyal once she does.

📝 Call Plan Worksheet — Dr. Hana (Social)

1 My Primary Objective for this Call
2 My Planned Opening Statement
Hint: Start with a personal, warm connection before any product mention. Show genuine interest in how she and her patients are doing.
3 Key Messages & Benefits to Highlight
Hint: Focus on patient comfort, safety profile, positive patient experience, and how it supports the trust between her and her patients.
4 Probing Questions to Ask
Hint: Ask about her patients' concerns, their experiences, and what matters most to her in choosing a treatment option for them.
5 How I Will Ask for Commitment
Hint: Offer your ongoing support — reassure her that you will be there for any questions. Frame commitment as a low-risk, caring choice for her patients.
My Personal Action Plan
Translating today's learnings into tomorrow's results

🧠 My Three Key Learnings

What are the three most important things you learned today that will change how you approach your work?

▶ START Doing

A new behavior or habit you will adopt starting from your very next customer call.

⏹ STOP Doing

A habit or default behavior that may be limiting your effectiveness — and that you commit to dropping.

🔄 CONTINUE Doing

A strength you already have that you want to consciously build on and do even better.

🌟

Thank You for Your Active Participation!

The workshop may be ending, but your journey of growth is just beginning. The true value of today's session lies in how you apply these learnings in every single customer interaction. Go out there and make every conversation count!

Behavioral Styles

Self-Assessment

A Self-Assessment Tool by Genio Talent Development®

This assessment will help you understand how you naturally think, work, and communicate with others.

It takes about 10 to 15 minutes. There are no right or wrong answers — be honest and choose what feels most natural to you.

How to Complete This Assessment

Please read carefully before you start

1
You will see 15 groups. Each group has 4 words. Each word describes a type of person.
2
Tap the word that BEST describes you. It will get a score of 4.
3
Then tap your second best (gets 3), then your third best (gets 2), then tap the least fitting word (gets 1). All 4 clicks are yours to make.
4
Tap all 4 words before moving to the next group — all 4 clicks are required. You cannot skip any group.
💡 Tip: Not sure what a word means? Tap the small ℹ button on the word to see a simple explanation.
🔄 Made a mistake? Tap any ranked word to undo from that point and start the group again.

Example — How It Looks

Friendly4 ★ Best Fit
Logical3 — Next Best
Creative2
Quiet1 ✗ Least Fit
Group 1 of 15

Which word best describes you?

Tap: Best fit (4) → Next (3) → Next (2) → Least fitting (1)

4
3
2
1
↩ Reset this group
